title: Documentation
selectSection: Select a section to get started

whatIs.title: What is DataForge?
whatIs.content:
DataForge is a backend platform that lets you build applications without writing server code.

Imagine you're creating a game, a website, or a mobile app. You need somewhere to store data — player profiles, orders, articles, scores. You also need a way for your app to read and write that data. That's exactly what DataForge does.

You create tables (like spreadsheets), and DataForge automatically gives you a secure API — a set of URLs your app can call to create, read, update, and delete data.

No need to set up servers, write database queries, or worry about security. DataForge handles all of that.

whoIsFor.title: Who is it for?
whoIsFor.content:
DataForge is built for anyone who needs a backend:

Game developers — Store player data, leaderboards, inventories, and game settings. Connect your game server directly to DataForge's API.

Web & mobile developers — Build apps faster by skipping the backend. Focus on your frontend while DataForge handles data storage, authentication, and APIs.

Small teams — Don't have a backend developer? DataForge gives you a production-ready backend with a visual interface. No code required for basic operations.

Prototyping — Need to test an idea quickly? Create a project, add some tables, and you have a working API in minutes.

Internal tools — Build dashboards, admin panels, and data management interfaces for your team.

quickStart.title: Quick Start Guide
quickStart.content:
Get your first API running in 5 minutes:

1. Create a project — Click "New Project", give it a name like "my-app".

2. Create a table — Go to Tables, click "New Table". Add columns like "name" (text), "email" (text), "age" (integer). Think of it as creating a spreadsheet.

3. Add some data — Click on your table, then "New Record". Fill in the fields and save. You can also import data from a CSV file.

4. Get your API — Go to API Endpoints. DataForge has already created endpoints for your table. You'll see URLs like GET /api/v1/my-app/users.

5. Create an API key — Go to Settings → API Tokens. Create a token — this is the password your app will use to access the data.

6. Start using it — From your app, call the API:

fetch('https://your-server/api/v1/my-app/users', {
  headers: { 'X-API-Key': 'your-token-here' }
})

That's it. You have a working backend.

projectBasics.title: What is a project?
projectBasics.content:
A project is your workspace. It contains everything for one application — tables, API endpoints, users, settings, and plugins.

Think of it like a folder for your app's backend. If you're building a game and a website, you'd create two separate projects. Each one is completely isolated — they don't share data or settings.

Every project gets:
• Its own database — Your tables and data are private to this project
• Its own API — Unique URLs for accessing data
• Its own users — Invite team members with different permissions
• Its own plugins — Enable only what you need

roles.title: Team roles and permissions
roles.content:
When you work with a team, not everyone needs the same level of access. DataForge has three roles:

Admin — Can do everything. Create tables, change settings, manage users, configure plugins. This is for project owners and lead developers.

Editor — Can work with data and APIs. Create records, edit endpoints, set up webhooks. Cannot change project settings or manage other users. This is for developers and content managers.

Viewer — Can only look at data and documentation. Cannot change anything. This is for stakeholders, QA testers, or anyone who just needs to see the data.

To invite someone, go to Settings → Invite Keys. Create an invite with the role you want, and share the code with your team member.

dashboard.title: Project dashboard
dashboard.content:
The dashboard is the first thing you see when you open a project. It gives you a quick overview:

• How many tables you have and how much data is stored
• A chart showing API requests over the last 7 days — so you can see how much your app is being used
• Recent activity — who changed what and when

It's your starting point for navigating the project.

tables.title: Creating and managing tables
tables.content:
Tables are where your data lives. If you've ever used a spreadsheet, tables work the same way — rows and columns.

To create a table, go to Tables and click "New Table". Give it a name (like "users" or "products") and add columns.

Each column has a type that defines what kind of data it stores:
• text — Words and sentences (names, descriptions, emails)
• integer — Whole numbers (age, count, score)
• boolean — Yes or no (is_active, is_verified)
• timestamp — Date and time (created_at, last_login)
• uuid — A unique ID that's automatically generated for each row
• jsonb — Complex data structures (settings, metadata)

Every table automatically gets an "id" column — a unique identifier for each row. You can also add:
• Default values — So you don't have to fill in every field manually
• Required fields — Fields that can't be left empty
• Unique constraints — Fields where no two rows can have the same value (like email)
• Foreign keys — Links between tables (like connecting an order to a customer)

dataBrowser.title: Working with data
dataBrowser.content:
The Data Browser is where you view, add, edit, and delete records.

When you open a table, you see all your data in a grid. Here's what you can do:

Search — Type in the search box to find records across all columns. Looking for a specific user? Just type their name.

Sort — Click any column header to sort ascending or descending. Want to see the newest records first? Sort by created_at.

Edit — Click on any cell to edit it directly. Or click on a row to open the full edit form.

Delete — Select rows with checkboxes and delete them in bulk.

Test data — Need sample data to test your app? Click "Generate test data" and DataForge will create realistic fake records for you.

Multiple views — Some data is better viewed differently. Enable the Kanban plugin to see tasks as cards, Calendar for events, or Gallery for images.

importExport.title: Importing and exporting data
importExport.content:
Already have data somewhere else? Import it.

Import supports CSV and JSON files. Upload your file, map the columns to your table fields, preview the data, and import. DataForge handles the rest.

Export lets you download your data as CSV or JSON. Useful for backups, sharing data with others, or migrating to another system.

You can export all records or just the ones you've filtered.

restApi.title: How the API works
restApi.content:
An API is how your application talks to DataForge. Instead of opening the website and clicking around, your app sends requests to specific URLs.

When you create a table called "users", DataForge automatically creates these API endpoints:

GET /api/v1/your-project/users — Get a list of all users
GET /api/v1/your-project/users/123 — Get one specific user
POST /api/v1/your-project/users — Create a new user
PUT /api/v1/your-project/users/123 — Update a user
DELETE /api/v1/your-project/users/123 — Delete a user

Your app calls these URLs with the data it wants to send. DataForge processes the request, updates the database, and sends back the result.

All endpoints support pagination (don't load 10,000 records at once), filtering (only get what you need), and sorting.

apiTokens.title: API keys and authentication
apiTokens.content:
You don't want anyone to be able to read or change your data. That's why DataForge requires an API key for most endpoints.

An API key is like a password for your application. When your app makes a request, it includes the key in the header:

X-API-Key: df_live_abc123...

To create a key, go to Settings → API Tokens. You can:
• Set permissions — Read-only keys can't modify data. Read-write keys can do everything.
• Set expiration — Keys can expire after a date. Good for temporary access.
• Restrict by IP — Only allow requests from specific IP addresses. Extra security for production.
• Revoke instantly — If a key is compromised, disable it with one click.

Never share your API key publicly. Don't put it in frontend JavaScript code that users can see. Keep it on your server.

apiDocs.title: API documentation and testing
apiDocs.content:
DataForge auto-generates documentation for your API. Go to API Documentation to see every endpoint with:
• What parameters it accepts
• What data it returns
• Example requests and responses

This documentation updates automatically when you change your tables or endpoints. Share it with your team or external developers who need to integrate with your API.

Want to test quickly? Enable the API Playground plugin for an interactive testing environment right in the browser.

pluginSystem.title: How plugins work
pluginSystem.content:
Not every project needs every feature. That's why DataForge uses plugins — you enable only what you need.

Go to Settings → Plugins to see three categories:

Views — Add new ways to see your data (Kanban boards, calendars, galleries)
Modules — Add backend features (scheduled tasks, webhooks, GraphQL)
Integrations — Connect to external services (Discord, Telegram, monitoring)

When you enable a plugin, it appears in the sidebar navigation. When you disable it, it's completely gone — no extra load, no clutter.

Some plugins are enabled by default because most projects need them (Analytics, Cron Jobs, Backups). Everything else is opt-in.

pluginAnalytics.title: Analytics
pluginAnalytics.content:
Problem it solves: You have an API, but you have no idea how it's being used. How many requests per day? Which endpoints are popular? Are there errors?

Analytics tracks every API request to your project and shows you:
• Total requests, average response time, error rate, and unique clients over 7 days
• Which endpoints are called most often
• Slow requests that might need optimization
• A full log of every request with method, path, status, and IP

This is enabled by default. You don't need to configure anything — just go to Analytics and see the data.

When to use: Always. If your app has users, you want to know how they use your API.

pluginCron.title: Cron Jobs (Scheduled tasks)
pluginCron.content:
Problem it solves: Some things need to happen automatically on a schedule. Clean up old data every week. Generate a daily report. Check for stale records every hour.

Cron Jobs let you run SQL queries on a schedule. For example:
• Delete inactive users every Sunday: DELETE FROM users WHERE last_login < NOW() - INTERVAL '90 days'
• Count daily signups every morning: INSERT INTO daily_stats SELECT COUNT(*) FROM users WHERE created_at >= CURRENT_DATE

You define the schedule using cron expressions (like "every day at 3 AM" or "every Monday at noon"), write the SQL, and DataForge runs it automatically.

Each job keeps a history — you can see when it last ran, whether it succeeded, and what errors occurred.

When to use: When you need regular data maintenance, automated reports, or periodic cleanups.

pluginWebhooks.title: Webhooks
pluginWebhooks.content:
Problem it solves: You want your external system to know when data changes. When a new user signs up, you want to send a welcome email. When an order is placed, you want to notify your fulfillment system.

Webhooks send an HTTP request to a URL you specify whenever data changes in selected tables. You choose:
• Which tables to watch
• Which events to listen for (new record created, record updated, record deleted)
• Where to send the notification (any URL)
• A secret for security verification

When the event happens, DataForge sends a POST request with the full record data to your URL. Your system receives it and does whatever it needs to do.

When to use: When you need to connect DataForge to other systems — email services, payment processors, notification systems, or your own backend.

pluginGraphql.title: GraphQL
pluginGraphql.content:
Problem it solves: REST APIs always return all fields. If your user table has 20 columns but you only need the name, you're wasting bandwidth. GraphQL lets you ask for exactly what you need.

When enabled, DataForge auto-generates a GraphQL schema from your tables. You can query like this:

{ users(limit: 5) { id, nickname } }

This returns only the id and nickname — nothing else. GraphQL is especially useful for mobile apps where bandwidth matters, or for complex queries that would require multiple REST calls.

Access it at /api/v1/your-project/graphql with your API key.

When to use: When your app needs flexible queries, when you want to reduce data transfer, or when you're building a modern frontend that benefits from GraphQL.

pluginWebsocket.title: WebSocket (Real-time)
pluginWebsocket.content:
Problem it solves: Your app needs live updates. A chat app, a live dashboard, a multiplayer game — you can't keep refreshing the page to see new data.

WebSocket gives your app a persistent connection to DataForge. Subscribe to table changes and receive updates instantly — no polling, no delays.

How it works:
1. Your app connects via WebSocket with an API key
2. It subscribes to a channel (e.g., "table:messages")
3. Whenever someone creates, updates, or deletes a record in that table, your app receives the change immediately

The WebSocket page shows connection stats and lets you configure which table events to track.

When to use: Chat apps, live dashboards, multiplayer games, collaborative tools — anything where users need to see changes in real-time.

pluginSdk.title: SDK (Code generation)
pluginSdk.content:
Problem it solves: Writing API calls manually is tedious and error-prone. You need to remember URLs, handle authentication, parse responses.

SDK generates ready-to-use client code for your API in three languages:
• TypeScript — A typed class with methods like createUsers(), getUsers(), etc.
• Python — A client class using the requests library
• cURL — Copy-paste commands for testing in terminal

The code updates automatically when your endpoints change. Copy it into your project and start using it immediately.

When to use: When you want to save time integrating with the API, especially when working with TypeScript or Python.

pluginPlayground.title: API Playground
pluginPlayground.content:
Problem it solves: You want to test your API without writing code or using external tools like Postman.

API Playground is a built-in tool for sending requests to any URL. Choose the HTTP method, enter a URL, add headers and body, and click Send. See the response instantly.

You can save requests to collections for reuse, and it keeps a history of your last 50 requests.

When to use: Quick API testing, debugging, exploring third-party APIs.

pluginDbMap.title: Database Map
pluginDbMap.content:
Problem it solves: When you have many tables with relationships, it's hard to see the big picture. Which table connects to which? Where are the foreign keys?

DB Map shows your entire database as an interactive diagram. Tables are boxes, foreign keys are arrows. You can zoom, pan, search, and click on any table to see its columns and indexes.

When to use: When your project has more than a few tables and you need to understand or explain the data model.

pluginQueryBuilder.title: Query Builder
pluginQueryBuilder.content:
Problem it solves: You need to query data but don't know SQL.

Query Builder lets you build queries visually. Select a table, pick columns, add filters, set sorting — and the SQL is generated for you. You can run it directly or copy the SQL to learn from it.

When to use: When you need custom data queries but aren't comfortable writing SQL, or when you want to quickly explore data.

pluginKanban.title: Kanban Board
pluginKanban.content:
Problem it solves: You have a table with a status column (like "todo", "in progress", "done") and want to see records as cards you can drag between columns.

Kanban turns any table into a visual board. Pick a column that represents status, and your records become cards organized by status. Drag a card to a different column to update its status.

When to use: Task management, project tracking, order processing — any workflow with stages.

pluginCalendar.title: Calendar View
pluginCalendar.content:
Problem it solves: You have records with dates (events, deadlines, appointments) and want to see them on a calendar.

Calendar displays records on a date grid. You can see what's happening on any day, drag events to reschedule, and filter by category.

When to use: Event scheduling, deadline tracking, booking systems.

pluginGallery.title: Gallery View
pluginGallery.content:
Problem it solves: You have records with images and want to browse them visually instead of scanning a data grid.

Gallery shows records as cards with image thumbnails. Each card displays a title, description, and image. Great for browsing visual content.

When to use: Media libraries, product catalogs, portfolios, any table with image URLs.

pluginDashboards.title: Dashboards
pluginDashboards.content:
Problem it solves: You want to see key metrics at a glance — charts, counters, tables — without building a custom frontend.

Dashboards let you create visual overviews with drag-and-drop widgets. Add charts, counters, and SQL-powered widgets to see your data the way you want.

When to use: Business metrics, team overviews, operational dashboards.

pluginBackups.title: Backups
pluginBackups.content:
Problem it solves: Data loss. Accidental deletion, bugs, or corruption — you need to be able to go back.

Backups creates snapshots of your database that you can restore at any time. This is enabled by default because every project should have backups.

When to use: Always keep this enabled. You'll be glad you did when something goes wrong.

intDiscord.title: Discord Webhook
intDiscord.content:
Problem it solves: You want your team to know when something happens in your app — a new user signed up, an order was placed, a server went down — without checking DataForge manually.

Discord Webhook sends formatted messages to Discord channels whenever data changes in your tables.

How to set up:
1. In Discord: Server Settings → Integrations → Webhooks → Create Webhook → Copy URL
2. In DataForge: Enable the Discord plugin → Go to the Discord page → Create a webhook
3. Paste the Discord URL, select which tables and events to watch
4. Customize the message — use variables like {data.nickname} to include record values
5. Set conditions — only send when specific criteria are met (e.g., status = "error")

The message editor supports Discord markdown (bold, italic, code), custom embed colors, and a live preview so you can see exactly how it will look.

When to use: Team notifications, moderation alerts, monitoring, any situation where your team lives in Discord.

intTelegram.title: Telegram Bot
intTelegram.content:
Problem it solves: Same as Discord, but for teams that use Telegram. Get instant mobile notifications when data changes.

How to set up:
1. Open Telegram, find @BotFather, create a bot, copy the token
2. Find your Chat ID (use @userinfobot)
3. Enable the Telegram plugin → Create a notification
4. Enter your Bot Token and Chat ID
5. Select tables, events, and customize the message template

Telegram supports HTML formatting — bold, italic, code blocks. You can use the same variables as Discord ({event}, {table}, {data.column}).

Conditions work the same way — send only when certain fields match your criteria.

When to use: Mobile alerts, personal notifications, teams that prefer Telegram over Discord.

intUptime.title: Uptime Monitor
intUptime.content:
Problem it solves: You have servers, APIs, or websites that need to stay online. You need to know immediately when something goes down.

Uptime Monitor automatically checks your URLs at regular intervals and logs the results.

How to set up:
1. Enable the plugin → Go to the Monitoring page
2. Create a monitor: enter a URL, choose how often to check (every 1 minute to every 12 hours)
3. Set the expected response (HTTP 200 OK by default)
4. Organize monitors into categories (Production, Staging, External)

The monitoring page shows:
• A sidebar with all monitors grouped by category, with UP/DOWN indicators
• Uptime percentage over 24 hours and 7 days
• Average response time
• A visual timeline bar (green = up, red = down, gray = no data)
• A detailed log of every check

All logs are stored in your project's uptime_logs table. This means you can:
• See the data in the Tables view
• Subscribe a Discord or Telegram webhook to uptime_logs with the condition is_up = false — and get instant alerts when something goes down

Logs are automatically cleaned up based on your retention setting (1-7 days).

When to use: Monitoring production services, external APIs, websites, or any HTTP endpoint that needs to be available.

intSboxAuth.title: S&box Authentication
intSboxAuth.content:
Problem it solves: You're building a game with S&box and need to verify that players are who they claim to be.

This plugin validates player tokens through the Facepunch API and manages game sessions. It maps Steam IDs to your database records so you can store per-player data.

When to use: S&box game development where you need player authentication and session management.

exGameServer.title: Example: Game server backend
exGameServer.content:
You're making a multiplayer game and need to store player profiles and leaderboards.

Step 1 — Create a project called "my-game"

Step 2 — Create two tables:
• players — columns: nickname (text), steam_id (text, unique), level (integer, default 1)
• scores — columns: player_id (uuid, foreign key → players), score (integer), map (text)

Step 3 — Go to API Endpoints. DataForge already created CRUD endpoints for both tables. Set authentication to api_token.

Step 4 — Create an API token in Settings → API Tokens.

Step 5 — From your game server:
• Register a player: POST /api/v1/my-game/players with {"nickname": "Player1", "steam_id": "765..."}
• Submit a score: POST /api/v1/my-game/scores with {"player_id": "...", "score": 1500, "map": "arena"}
• Get leaderboard: GET /api/v1/my-game/scores (sorted by score)

Optional plugins:
• Enable S&box Auth for Steam-based authentication
• Enable Discord Webhook to announce new high scores in your server
• Enable Analytics to see how many API requests your game makes

Result: A complete game backend with authentication, leaderboards, and notifications — without writing any server code.

exMonitoring.title: Example: Full monitoring system
exMonitoring.content: You run several services and need to know when something breaks. Step 1 — Create a project called "monitoring" Step 2 — Enable Uptime Monitor. Create monitors: • "API Server" → https://api.yourapp.com/health (every 1 minute) • "Website" → https://yourapp.com (every 5 minutes) • "Payment API" → https://api.stripe.com (every 15 minutes) Organize them into categories: Production, External. Step 3 — Enable Discord Webhook. Create a webhook: • Watch the uptime_logs table for INSERT events • Add condition: is_up equals false • Set embed title: "{data.monitor_name} is DOWN" • Set color to red Now whenever a service goes down, your Discord channel gets an instant alert. Step 4 — Enable Telegram Bot for mobile alerts: • Same setup: watch uptime_logs, condition is_up = false • Template: "{data.monitor_name} is DOWN — {data.reason}" Step 5 — Enable Cron Jobs for a daily summary: • Schedule: every day at 9 AM • SQL: SELECT monitor_name, ROUND(100.0 * COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE is_up) / COUNT(*), 2) as uptime_percent FROM uptime_logs WHERE checked_at >= NOW() - INTERVAL '24 hours' GROUP BY monitor_name ORDER BY uptime_percent Result: Automatic monitoring with instant alerts on Discord and Telegram, mobile notifications, and daily reports. If your API goes down at 3 AM, you'll know before your users do.
